summary: I was born and spent my early childhood in San Bernardino, California. We lived there until I was in the fifth grade before moving to Pomona. It was during the Depression, so jobs were scarce, but it was home during my formative years.

I enlisted in the Army Air Corps right after the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941. I trained as a bombardier and served in the Eighth Air Force during World War II. I stayed in the service through the Korean and Vietnam Wars until I retired in 1967.

During World War II, I served as a bombardier with the Eighth Air Force. I flew B-24 missions over Europe, including raids in North Africa and Italy. It was a challenging time, but I felt a strong sense of duty and camaraderie with my crew.

I was assigned to the Eighth Air Force during World War II, flying B-24 bombers on missions over Europe. We trained extensively in the U.S. before deploying to England and later North Africa. The Eighth Air Force was a major part of my military career until I retired.

I remained in the Air Force through the Vietnam War era, although my primary combat experience was during World War II. I served in various roles within the Air Force until my retirement in 1967. The Vietnam War was part of the broader context of my military career.

After World War II, I continued my service through the Korean War period. I was part of the Fifteenth and Eighth Air Forces during that time. My military career spanned multiple conflicts until I retired in 1967.

Following World War II, I served in the Fifteenth Air Force as part of my continued military career. This was during the Korean and Vietnam War periods before I retired in 1967. The Fifteenth Air Force was one of the units I was proud to be part of.

My family moved to Pomona, California when I was in the fifth grade, and that's where I grew up. I attended Pomona Junior College and later La Verne College. Pomona was where I spent my youth and early adulthood before joining the military.

I attended Pomona Junior College, which had a pilot training program that many of my friends joined. It was a stepping stone before I enlisted in the Army Air Corps. Pomona College helped prepare me for my military career.

After Pomona Junior College, I went on to graduate from La Verne College. It took me a while to finish college because I wasn't a strong student, but I enjoyed sports like baseball and football. La Verne was an important part of my education before the war.

I vividly remember hearing on the radio about the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor in December 1941. That news motivated me to enlist in the Army Air Corps the very next day at March Field. It was a turning point that changed my life forever.

My older brother served in the United States Navy during the war. He was a great aviator, and although I was in the Air Corps, we shared a strong family commitment to service. Sadly, he passed away when we were young.

I enlisted at March Air Force Base in Riverside, California, right after Pearl Harbor. That was where I took the tests and interviews to get into the cadet program. It was the starting point of my military aviation career.

Instead of going directly to primary flight training, I was sent to Santa Ana Army Air Base in California for about three months. This was part of the new policy where all cadets had to pass through there. It was an important step before I went to Phoenix for primary training.

I went to Phoenix, Arizona, for my primary flight training after Santa Ana. This was where I initially trained as a pilot but eventually washed out. Phoenix was a key location in my early military training.

During bombardier training in New Mexico, I trained extensively on the Norden bombsight. It was a remarkable piece of equipment, and I learned to use it so well that my bombing accuracy was within 100 feet. It was a closely guarded secret, and I had to personally sign it out every time.

I spent about seven to eight months in New Mexico for bombardier training during World War II. This is where I graduated on December 30, 1942. New Mexico was crucial in preparing me for combat missions.

After graduating bombardier school, I was sent to Davis-Monthan Air Force Base in Tucson, Arizona. There, I joined my B-24 crew and began forming our team for combat. Tucson was where our crew came together before heading overseas.

Davis-Monthan Air Force Base in Tucson was where I joined my B-24 crew after bombardier training. We trained together there before being declared combat ready. It was the last stop stateside before deployment.

Our crew underwent further training in Alamogordo, New Mexico, as part of preparing for combat missions. This was one of the locations where we practiced before being sent overseas. Alamogordo was an important training site for bomber crews.

After training, our crew went to Topeka, Kansas, where we picked up our B-24 bomber. From there, we flew the aircraft as a crew to England to join the Eighth Air Force. Topeka was the final stateside stop before deployment to Europe.

We flew our B-24 from Topeka to England, arriving first near Edinburgh where the planes were modified. Then we took a train through London to our base near The Wash. England was our base for training and launching missions in Europe during World War II.

Our first stop in the UK was near Edinburgh, where new airplanes were taken in for modifications. We left our plane there before traveling south to our base in England. Edinburgh was an important logistical point for our deployment.

After arriving in Scotland, I took a train through London on the way to our base. I decided to stay overnight in London, thinking I might never return. It was a memorable moment before joining my crew at the base near The Wash.

Our airbase was located near The Wash, a coastal area in England. From there, our crew began low-level training missions in preparation for combat. The Wash was the home base for our group during much of our time in England.

Our low-level training in England was in preparation for the Ploiești raids, which were planned missions against oil refineries in Romania. We trained flying just above the trees to simulate the attack approach. Ploiești was a key target in our strategic bombing efforts.

In May 1943, our crew moved to North Africa, flying missions out of Benghazi. We participated in bombing runs over Italy as part of the North Africa Campaign. It was a critical phase of the war and our combat experience.

Benghazi in North Africa was our base for several combat missions in 1943. From there, we flew bombing raids into Italy, including a notable mission over Rome. Benghazi was a strategic location during the North Africa Campaign.

We flew bombing missions over Italy, including targeting the marshalling yards in Rome to disrupt rail traffic. These missions were flown from North Africa and were part of the broader European air campaign. Italy was a key target in our efforts.

One of our most notable missions was bombing the marshalling yards in Rome, Italy. The rail yards were crucial for transportation, and hitting them was strategically important. Flying over Rome was a significant and memorable combat experience.

During a mission, our crew encountered a German jet fighter, the Messerschmitt Me 262. It was the first jet aircraft I had ever seen, and it was incredibly fast with no propeller. Our top gunner shot it down, which was a remarkable moment in combat.

Romania was the location of the Ploiești oil refineries, which were a major target for our low-level bombing raids. Our training in England was geared toward these missions. Romania's oil production was vital to the Axis war effort, making it a critical target.

I married Joella Woods during a two-week leave before deploying overseas. Joella was from Idaho, and we had known each other since childhood, though we only started dating shortly before I went into the cadet program. She was a great support to me throughout the war.

Joella Woods, my wife, was originally from Idaho, specifically from a small town called Beulah. Although I was from California, our paths crossed early in life, and she became my wife just before I went overseas. Idaho was important to her background.

On our way to England, we flew via Greenland as part of the ferry route for new aircraft. It was a stopover point before reaching Scotland and then England. Greenland was a key link in the transatlantic flight path during the war.
